coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional CuS-based micro-flower loaded with carbon dots/laccase for effectively detection and removal of catechol
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Shuxing
Wang, Yin
Cao, Wenyu
coredata.dc:description: The miniaturization and autonomous mobility of the artificial micro-composite have gained widespread interest and attention in the field of catechol treatment. Unfortunately, it is a bottleneck for micro-composite in realizing the integration of multiple functions such as colorimetric detection and removal of catechol. Herein, we construct a Janus micro-composite with dual functions of detection and degradation for the efficient detection and removal of catechol. The Janus micro-composite for laccase immobilization was prepared by growing manganese dioxide nanoparticles (MnO2 NPs) on the hemisphere of copper sulfide (CuS) loaded with carbon (C) dots (Lac-MnO2@C@CuS). The half-coated MnO2 NPs is used as a power source to generate oxygen (O2), which drives the Janus micro-composite for autonomous directional motion at a speed of 178.83 ± 2.07 μm/s in the presence of 5 wt% H2O2. The Janus micro-composite with excellent peroxidase-like properties could display sensitive detection of catechol with the detection limits of 0.49 μM in range of 0–100 μM. Furthermore, the Janus micro-composite could decompose H2O2 to produce abundant reactive oxygen species (ROS) through Fenton reaction, which could promote the removal rate of catechol up to 96.6%. This is a promising micro-composite platform for integrated autonomous movement and simultaneous detection/removal of catechol, which can provide a new perspective for water environment treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The miniaturization and autonomous mobility of the artificial micro-composite have gained widespread interest and attention in the field of catechol treatment. Unfortunately, it is a bottleneck for micro-composite in realizing the integration of multiple functions such as colorimetric detection and removal of catechol. Herein, we construct a Janus micro-composite with dual functions of detection and degradation for the efficient detection and removal of catechol. The Janus micro-composite for laccase immobilization was prepared by growing manganese dioxide nanoparticles (MnO2 NPs) on the hemisphere of copper sulfide (CuS) loaded with carbon (C) dots (Lac-MnO2@C@CuS). The half-coated MnO2 NPs is used as a power source to generate oxygen (O2), which drives the Janus micro-composite for autonomous directional motion at a speed of 178.83 ± 2.07 μm/s in the presence of 5 wt% H2O2. The Janus micro-composite with excellent peroxidase-like properties could display sensitive detection of catechol with the detection limits of 0.49 μM in range of 0–100 μM. Furthermore, the Janus micro-composite could decompose H2O2 to produce abundant reactive oxygen species (ROS) through Fenton reaction, which could promote the removal rate of catechol up to 96.6%. This is a promising micro-composite platform for integrated autonomous movement and simultaneous detection/removal of catechol, which can provide a new perspective for water environment treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Catechol is a phenolic compound that exists in nature in the form of a variety of derivatives (Baei et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021; X. Chen et al., 2022; D. Li et al., 2021). Catechol as an important chemical raw material has been widely used in pesticide, medicine, spices, dyes, photographic materials and rubber industries. After industrial production, a small amount of unreacted catechol will be discharged into the environment with organic solvents and water. Although catechol is widely used in production, it is also an important organic pollutant with high toxicity, which is difficult to degrade and seriously pollutes the environment and endangers human health. Therefore, it is of great significance for the detection and removal of catechol in natural environment.

In recent years, with the rapid development of nanotechnology, a large number of analytical technologies such as liquid chromatography (Wicht et al., 2022), electrochemical techniques (N.B. Li et al., 2021), photoluminescence (Ye et al., 2021), and spectrophotometry (Guan et al., 2022) have been gradually used to improve the detection rate of catechol. In addition, different water treatment methods such as membrane technology (Ahmad et al., 2023a,b; Ahmad et al., 2023a,b; Ahmad et al., 2023a,b; Ahmad et al., 2020), homogeneous Fenton treatment technology (Meng et al., 2022), physical adsorption (Gutiérrez Ortiz et al., 2019) and extraction (Drouin et al., 2016) have been used to remove and degrade catechol. Owing to its advantages of simple operation, low price, high selectively and visualization, colorimetric detection combined with homogeneous Fenton treatment technology is favored by researchers (Zhang et al., 2021; Sharma et al., 2021). Particularly, nanozyme has attracted much attention due to its environmental friendliness, simple operation technology and high cost efficiency (Y. Zhang et al., 2022; X. Li et al., 2021; Duan et al., 2022). Laccase is a copper-containing polyphenol oxidase, and the copper ion in its structure can oxidize the phenolic substrate. The whole catalytic reaction process is green and environmentally friendly. Therefore, laccase has the ability to catalyze the degradation of toxic and harmful phenols in the environment and can be used to remove catechol (Liang et al., 2022). However, free laccase as a biocatalyst is limited due to its poor stability and high cost. Therefore, immobilized laccase has attracted a lot of attention due to its ability to improve the resistance of enzyme molecules to denaturation conditions such as temperature, pH value and organic solvents (Isanapong and Pornwongthong, 2021).

As a typical p-type semiconductor material, copper sulfide (CuS) exhibits excellent photocatalytic activity and chemical properties due to its narrow band gap (∼2.1 eV) (Siddhardhan et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2021; Yuan et al., 2023). In addition, CuS is closely concerned as a kind of peroxidase-like catalyst to stimulate the decomposition of H2O2 to generate •OH (Borthakur et al., 2021; S. Wu et al., 2022). To improve the catalytic activity, nanoparticles are loaded on the surface of different support materials to form a new type of composite structural materials with excellent physical and chemical properties. Owing to their good stability, cheap availability, low biological toxicity and easy chemical modification, carbon dots with excellent peroxidase-like performance have attracted wide attention in detection and degradation of organic pollutants (Li et al., 2022, 2023; Yang et al., 2021; Su et al., 2022).

Synthetic micro/nano-materials can achieve autonomous motion by converting other chemical energy into in-situ mechanical kinetic energy, which has aroused widespread interest in water pollution treatment. The miniaturization of self-propelled micro-composite reduces its own quality, improves the mixing rate between the sample and the solution, accelerates the contact area between the adsorbent itself and the target pollutant, and thus improves the degradation ability of the pollutant (Sun et al., 2023; Y. Zhang et al., 2022; Luo et al., 2021). The preparation morphology of micro-composite mainly includes conical tube, Janus structure (Zhu et al., 2021), nanorod structure (Li et al., 2022), etc. Among them, the Janus structure is composed of two components with different chemical and physical properties and has been widely developed. However, most Janus structures are prepared by vacuum sputtering technology (Yang et al., 2018), which is limited in the field of pollutant removal due to its complex preparation process, high cost and low efficiency.

Herein, we prepared a novel CuS-based Janus micro-composite with autonomous propulsion for colorimetric sensitivity detection and removal of catechol. A thin layer of MnO2 NPs was embedded in the hemisphere of CuS to form a composite system including laccase, carbon dots, CuS and MnO2 NPs. In particular, as a 3D lamellar structure, CuS could provide a large number of active sites and big surface area for the loading of C dots and laccase, improving the activity and stability of laccase. Furthermore, MnO2 NPs could efficiently decompose H2O2 to produce O2 to drive the micro-composite for directional and autonomous propulsion. Otherwise, N doped C dots and CuS simultaneously could exhibit the superior peroxidase-like catalytic activity by Fenton-like reactions to produce ROS for the efficient and sensitive detection and removal of catechol. In brief, CuS-based Janus micro-composite with autonomous propulsion and superior peroxidase-like catalytic activity for colorimetric sensitivity detection and removal of catechol is expected to be widely used in environmental management after the production of pesticides, pharmaceuticals, spices, dyes, photographic materials, and rubber industries.

CuCl2•2H2O, thiourea, fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) and citric acid were acquired from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Urea and KMnO4 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) was provided from Sigma Aldrich. 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), NaN3, p-benzoquinone (BQ) and isopropanol (IPA) were purchased from Aladdin. CH3COONa (NaAc) and CH3COOH (HAc) were purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. H2O2 (30 wt%) was acquired from Yantai Far East Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Catechol and disodium EDTA (EDTA-2Na) was supplied by Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. Laccase was purchased from Beijing Solarbio Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Terephthalic acid (TA) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethylene glycol and ethanol were acquired from Damao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). All the purchased reagents were used directly.

The appearance and structure were displayed by field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, Gemini 300). The corresponding element mappings were analyzed from the Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-1400). The surface chemical composition of the obtained sample was measured by XPS (AlKα ray sources). FT-IR spectroscopy was measured by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet 380). The XRD patterns were performed by X-Ray Diffractometer (Cu Kα, 2θ range 10–80°). The absorbance of the obtained samples was measured by a U-4600 spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan). The adsorption-desorption curve and specific surface area of the obtained sample are displayed by a specific surface and porosity analyzer (ASAP2020M + C, USA), and the average pore volume was measured by the BJH model. The real-time images and motion videos of the micro-composite were photographed via Inverted microscope.

CuS microsphere was synthesized by previously reported hydrothermal method with minor modification (E. Ma et al., 2021). In brief, 0.3 g CuCl2•2H2O in 50 mL of ethylene glycol was stirred for 30 min until dissolved at room temperature. 0.7 g of thiourea was slowly injected into the above solution with vigorous stirring. After being stirred for 6 h at room temperature, the black product was transferred to Teflon-lined autoclave and hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 200 °C for 12 h. Until it was reduced to room temperature, the product was centrifuged and washed three times, and then dried in vacuum at 60 °C overnight. The black product was defined as CuS NPs.

Concretely, 0.25 g citric acid and 0.4 g urea were solved in 50 mL deionized water (Bogireddy et al., 2020). After being stirred for 20 min at room temperature, the clear solution was added into 30 mL CuS solution dropwise and stirred for 6 h at room temperature. After that, the mixture was transferred into the high-pressure reactor at 200 °C for 1 h. The obtained product was collected and centrifuged, and finally washed with water for three times. The final product was stored at 4 °C for further use. The obtained product was identified as C@CuS NPs.

Specifically, 0.3 g PMMA was dissolved in 10 mL ethyl acetate and stirred continuously at 60 °C for 30 min to form a clear solution. The PMMA solution was evenly coated on the slide to form a film, and then C@CuS NPs were dispersed over the PMMA film. KMnO4 solution containing NaOH was sprayed on the above slide, and the 100 W light was illuminated for 12 h. Then, the formed brown product was scraped off and washed several times until the supernatant became colorless. Finally, the product was freeze-dried overnight.

30 mg laccase in 20 mL deionized water was injected into 30 mL MnO2@C@CuS solution with vigorous stirring. After being stirred for 6 h at 30 °C, the obtained materials were collected and rinsed with deionized water several times to remove the free laccase. The product was freeze-dried for 12 h. The obtained samples were known as Lac-MnO2@C@CuS.

pH (3.6–7.0), temperature (25–55 °C), incubation time (2–10 min), TMB substrate concentration (0.05–0.5 mM), hydrogen peroxide concentration (10–200 mM) and catalyst concentration (0.01–0.08 mg/mL) have important effects on the peroxidase-like activity of the Janus micro-composite. Therefore, the peroxidase-like activity of Janus micro-composite was evaluated to achieve the optimal reaction condition.

Furthermore, to investigate the active species of peroxide-like enzyme during the colorimetric reaction process, IPA, BQ, NaN3 and EDTA-2Na were employed as indicators for O2 •-, •OH, 1O2 and holes (h+), respectively. Typically, IPA, BQ and EDTA-2Na solution (10 mM) were injected into the 0.1 mg/mL Janus micro-composite solution containing TMB (0.25 mM) and H2O2 (200 mM), respectively. The reaction system was incubated at 35 °C for 10 min. The absorption of the solution was measured at 652 nm.

In brief, 200 μL of TMB (0.25 mM), 100 μL of H2O2 (0.2 M) and 50 μL of Janus micro-composite suspension (0.1 mg/mL) were added into 2 mL of NaAc-HAc buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 6.0) and incubated at 35 °C for 10 min. Afterward, 300 μL catechol with different concentrations was added into the above colorimetric system and incubated for another 5 min. Finally, the solution after the reaction was rapidly centrifuged and the UV–vis absorption of the supernatant was detected.

To evaluate the selectivity of Janus micro-composite, 2,4-DCP, catechol, phenol, resorcinol and five common ions (Na+, Ca2+, CO3 2−, K+ and Cl−) were measured and evaluated. The final concentration of catechol was 0.1 mM, while concentration of other interfering ions was 1.0 mM.

In brief, Janus micro-composite (4.0 mg) were dispersed into catechol solution (40 mL, 0.01 mg/mL, pH = 6.0). Certain concentration H2O2 was added into the above solution. Next, the reaction mixture (2.0 mL) was sucked out at different times. Finally, the solution was rapidly centrifuged and the absorbance of supernatant was recorded at 652 nm, respectively. The removal efficiency η of Janus micro-composite to catechol can be calculated by Equation (1) (Liu et al., 2023; Y. Chen et al., 2022):
(1)

%
Where

(μg/mL) was the initial concentration of catechol solution, and

(μg/mL) represented the concentration of catechol at time t.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reusability of Janus micro-composite
ce:section.ce:para: The cycle and stability of the peroxidase-like activity of the Janus micro-composite were evaluated by colorimetric reaction. Typically, after each sample use, the collected material was rinsed with NaAc-HAc buffer solution and dried before being used for the next cycle of testing. The specific steps of colorimetric detection and removal experiments are consistent with those described in Sections 2.8 and 2.9.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of lac-Janus micro-composite
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process of the lac-Janus micro-composite was displayed in Scheme 1 . Firstly, CuS NPs were fabricated via a facile hydrothermal synthesis method. Then, N-doped C dots were fixed on the surface of CuS NPs by a simple hydrothermal method. Subsequently, Janus micro-composite was prepared by coating KMnO4 solution on the hemisphere of C@CuS and forming MnO2 NPs by simple light reduction method. Finally, laccase was immobilized on the surface of Janus micro-composite by electrostatic adsorption. The semi-coated MnO2 nanoparticles could decompose H2O2 efficiently to produce O2 through catalase-like activity to form autonomous motion. In addition, the Janus micro-composite could generate •OH and O2 •- via a Fenton-like reaction to efficiently detect and remove catechol simultaneously.

The morphology of CuS and Janus micro-composite were described by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a–b, the shape of CuS showed flower-like microsphere with an average diameter of 8 μm, which was consisted of nanosheets, and the Janus micro-composite retained the structure of CuS microsphere. Importantly, the large surface area of CuS provided stable and abundant active sites for C dots fixation and laccase immobilization. MnO2 NPs with a mean diameter of 100 nm was fixed on the hemisphere of C dots loaded CuS microsphere, creating the semi-clad Janus micro-composite. To further investigate preparation of the Janus micro-composite successfully, the HAADF-STEM and EDS mappings also were observed. Fig. 1c and Fig. 1g-l displayed the presence and homogeneous distribution of Cu, S, C and O elements, and the distribution of Mn element in the hemispherical surface of the CuS nanoflower-like microsphere. It can also be seen from the green parts (Fig. 1a) and pink parts (Fig. 1b) that MnO2 NPs were asymmetrically distributed on the hemispheres of CuS nanoflower-like microsphere. As shown in Fig. 1d–e, the lattice fringe image of C dots could be observed by HRTEM, showing the 111 crystal plane of the C dots, with a lattice spacing of 0.200 nm. This lattice spacing was consistent with diamond-like carbon (Bogireddy et al., 2020). In order to demonstrate that laccase was immobilized onto the surface of the Janus micro-composite, FITC was selected to label the laccase. As descripted in Fig. 1f, the laccase labeled by the FITC showed obvious green fluorescence, which also revealed that the laccase molecules were immobilized on the surface of the Janus micro-composite.

The crystal structure of the C@CuS and Janus micro-composite was further characterized by XRD. As manifested in Fig. 2 a, the Janus micro-composite retained the characteristic peaks belonging to CuS and MnO2. For C@CuS, these characteristic diffraction peaks were assigned to (100), (101), (102), (103), (110), (108) and (116) planes of standard diffraction pattern of CuS NPs (PDF# 06–0464), respectively (Li et al., 2022). For MnO2@C@CuS, besides the standard CuS characteristic diffraction peaks, three new characteristic peaks around 37.9°, 46.8°, 54.9° were found, which belong to standard diffraction pattern of MnO2 NPs (PDF#12–0141). These results could confirm that MnO2 NPs have been successfully reduced on the surface of CuS microspore. The porosity and surface area of Janus micro-composite were measured by N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm. As shown in Fig. 2b, the Janus micro-composite were a typical type-IV adsorption isotherm with a slight H3 hysteretic singlet in the area of moderate relative pressure, indicating mesoporous formation. Furthermore, as shown Fig. 2c, the corresponding pore size of Janus micro-composite was about 12.3 nm. The result could reveal that the obtained Janus micro-composite were mesoporous structure. In addition, the Janus micro-composite had the BET specific surface area of approximately 179.8 m2/g, which could help to expose more active sites and improve catalytic performance. Furthermore, it could demonstrate that the Janus micro-composite had had the potential to absorb a large amount of pollutants.

The surface element valence of the Janus micro-composite was also analyzed by XPS (Fig. 2d–i). Fig. 2d represented the XPS spectrum of MnO2@C@CuS, confirming the existence of elements such as Cu, Mn, O, N, C and S in the Janus micro-composite. For the Cu 2p, two distinctive peaks of Cu 2p at ∼952.52 eV and ∼932.61 eV assigned to Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2, respectively. In addition, a distinct satellite peak appeared at ∼942.85 eV, which was attributed to Cu2+ (Fig. 2e) (Li et al., 2022). For Mn 2p spectrum, the characteristic peaks at ∼652.54 eV and ∼642.34 eV corresponding to Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2 was observed, respectively. Furthermore, an obviously satellite peak appeared at ∼648.56 eV, which was belonged to Mn2+ (Fig. 2f) (L. Wu et al., 2022; Z. Ma et al., 2021). What's more, the area covered by Mn4+ was higher than that of Mn2+, which indirectly provided the possibility for MnO2 NPs to display peroxidase-like catalytic activity. For the C 1s spectrum, the typical characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.9 eV and 288.9 eV correspond to C-C, C-N and CO bonds respectively, implying the existence of carbon-containing groups in the Janus micro-composite (Fig. 2g). Moreover, as shown in Fig. 2h, the characteristic peak identified as O 1s were considered as the C-O bond and Mn-O bond for MnO2 (∼529.6 eV). For S 2p spectrum, the S 2p spectra descripted displayed two peaks belonging to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 and their shake-up satellites (Fig. 2i).

In addition, the immobilization of laccase had also been further investigated using the coomassie brilliant blue method (Tran et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. S1, with the increase of laccase concentration, the UV absorption value at 595 nm also increased. the immobilization rate of laccase was about 79.56% by the following equation (2) (Wang et al., 2022):
(2)
E =
C 0
− C
C 0
× 100
%
Such high laccase immobilization rate could provide the possibility for the Janus micro-composite to display high catalytic activity and simultaneously detect and degrade catechol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on motion behavior of Janus micro-composite
ce:section.ce:para: As descripted in Fig. 3 , the driving behavior of the Janus micro-composite was also explored in detail. As shown in Fig. 3a–c, the time-lapse images and motion tracking track of Lac-MnO2@C@CuS Janus micro-composite in 1 wt%, 3 wt% and 5 wt% H2O2 solution with an interval of 3 s was captured by the motion video exhibited in Video S1-S3 . Obviously, a mass of bubbles was produced during the movement of the Janus micro-composite. With the extension of time to 12 s, the motion trajectory of the Janus micro-composite was also increasing in the presence of 1 wt%, 3 wt% and 5 wt% H2O2 solution, respectively. In addition, the motion trajectories of the Lac-MnO2@C@CuS Janus micro-composite were positively correlated with the concentration of H2O2.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2023.139939

The following are the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2
Multimedia component 3 Multimedia component 3
Multimedia component 4 Multimedia component 4

In addition, the effect of H2O2 on the motion velocity of the Janus micro-composite was shown in Fig. 3d. It was a positive correlation between the speed of Janus micro-composite and the concentration of hydrogen peroxide. The driving speed of the Janus micro-composite was 50.23 ± 2.3 μm/s, 123.42 ± 2.2 μm/s and 178.52 ± 2.1 μm/s with an assistance of 1 wt%, 3 wt% and 5 wt% H2O2 solution, respectively. It was speculated that the main reason for this result was that with the increase of H2O2 solution concentration, MnO2 could decompose H2O2 to produce more oxygen bubbles, thus generating greater reverse driving force to form a higher motion speed. Meanwhile, as shown in Table S1, the speeds of other designed micromotors had also been further compared with Lac-MnO2@C@CuS Janus micro-composite, and it was evident that Lac-MnO2@C@CuS Janus micro-composite had higher speeds than previously reported micromotors.

Moreover, the autonomous driving mechanism of the Janus micro-composite was also described in the Fig. 3e. The autonomous motion of the Janus micro-composite was mainly caused by the decomposition of H2O2 by MnO2 NPs to produce O2 with the assistance of hydrogen peroxide. The specific reaction process was described in Equation (3):
(3)
H 2
O 2
→
M n
O 2
O 2
↑ +
H 2
O

The asymmetric structure formed by the growth of MnO2 NPs on the hemispheres of CuS could promote the oxygen generated by decomposition to produce bubbles. The driving force could propel the Janus micro-composite to make directional motion in the opposite direction. The faster movement speed could increase the collision between the Janus micro-composite and the pollutants, and could improve the efficient detection and degradation of the organic pollutant catechol.

The peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the Lac-MnO2@C@CuS Janus micro-composite was investigated by selecting TMB as the chromogenic substrate (Wang et al., 2022; Y. Li et al., 2021). The peroxidase-like catalytic activity of each component contained in the Lac-MnO2@C@CuS Janus micro-composite was studied as a control group. As shown in Fig. 4 a, under the weakly acidic condition of pH 6.0, with the help of H2O2, the UV absorption of TMB + CuS + H2O2 group (1), TMB + C@CuS + H2O2 group (2) and TMB + MnO2@C@CuS + H2O2 group (3) increased successively. However, the Janus micro-composite group (5) showed the most obvious UV absorption values, and the chromogenic system solution displayed the strongest blue, which also proved that the Janus micro-composite had the strongest catalytic activity of peroxide-like enzymes. This result further could confirm that C dots, MnO2 NPs and laccase could play an important role in improving the peroxidase-like activity of the whole Janus micro-composite system. Moreover, as a control, there was no absorption and no significant color change at 652 nm in TMB + CuS group (4) without the help of H2O2, indicating that CuS had no oxidase-like catalytic properties. The Janus micro-composite that immobilized laccase showed strong peroxidase-like catalytic activity, which was mainly due to the special lamellar structure of CuS and the high catalytic active sites on the surface, which facilitated the high immobilization of laccase and the location of C dots.

Moreover, the peroxide-like catalytic activity of the Janus micro-composite is critically related to pH, temperature, catalyst concentration, H2O2 concentration, chromogenic substrate TMB and incubation time. As shown in Fig. S2a, when the concentration of H2O2 increased to 200 mM, the UV absorption value reached the maximum at 652 nm. With the change of pH, it was found that the most obvious condition for color development was under weak acid condition (Fig. S2b). In addition, temperature, TMB and incubation time also have great effects on the activity of peroxidase-like enzymes (Figs. S2c–f). Finally, the optimum reaction conditions were selected as pH 6.0, 200 mM, 0.25 mM, 10 min, 35 °C and 0.08 mg/mL, respectively.

Moreover, to investigate the active species that play an important role in the process of displaying the activity of peroxidase-like activity by Janus micro-composite, IPA, BQ, EDTA-2Na and NaN3 were used as trapping agents to capture •OH, O2 •-, h+ and 1O2, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4b, compared with the UV absorption intensity shown by the other three trapping agents, the UV absorption intensity of BQ trapping agent at 652 nm was the lowest, and the color of the solution showed a distinct blue fade. It was proved that O2 •- played an important role in the process of color development. In addition, when IPA was introduced into the colorimetric system, the UV absorption value at 652 nm also decreased significantly, and the color of the blue reaction system also changed, demonstrating that •OH were also captured in the process. Increase of benzoquinone could obviously inhibit the chromogenic reaction, confirming that the O2 •- could play a leading role in the catalytic reaction of TMB oxidation. In addition, to further confirm the generation of •OH, TA was used as a fluorescent indicator to trap the production of •OH. •OH was captured by TA and could generate 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (2-OH-TA) with strong fluorescence. As illustrated in Fig. 4c, the Janus micro-composite group showed the strongest fluorescence intensity in the presence of H2O2, demonstrating that •OH were generated through Fenton-like and peroxide-like catalytic activity in this reaction system. By contrast, the hydroxyl radicals produced by the components in the Janus micro-composite were also captured by TA, but the fluorescence intensity was weaker than that of the Janus micro-composite after laccase immobilization. This phenomenon could show that •OH could also play a crucial role in the system. It could also indirectly prove the high catalytic activity of the lac-Janus micro-composite and provide the possibility for the subsequent detection and removal of pollutants. The electron spin resonance (ESR) using DMPO as spin catcher were applied to further detect the production of •OH and O2 •- of active species in colorimetric systems. As shown in Fig. 4d, when the Janus micro-composite was introduced into the reaction system, the adduct DMPO/•OH was also detected, showing four obvious characteristic peaks, with peak intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1, further reflecting the formation of •OH. In addition, DMPO/O2 •- reaction system was also evaluated in the Janus micro-composite solution containing 0.5 wt% H2O2. ESR spectrum displayed four obvious characteristic peaks, with peak intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1, which further confirmed the generation of O2 •-. The above results all confirmed the existence of •OH and O2 •-. Based on the above results, the mechanism of •OH and O2 •- generation was further explored. As descripted in Fig. 4e, CuS and C dots could exhibit peroxidase-like catalytic activity to decompose H2O2 to produce •OH. MnO2 NPs could catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into O2 and water molecules. Meanwhile, on the one hand, the generated O2 molecules could be employed as the power source of the Janus micro-composite autonomous drive. On the other hand, MnO2 could decompose O2 into O2 •- by its oxidase-like catalytic property. In addition, the cyclic conversion between Mn4+/Mn2+ for MnO2 NPs could generate a large number of •OH via Fenton-like reaction. Moreover, Mn2+ was oxidized to Mn4+ by providing electrons to O2 to form O2 •- during the conversion process. Furthermore, laccase was a kind of polyphenol oxidase containing four copper ions, which could catalyze a variety of compounds such as mono, binary, polycheese, methoxy benzene, amino acid, aromatic amine, etc., and could carry out catalytic reactions through the transfer of electrons with four copper atoms. Thus, the components in the Janus micro-composite could jointly promote the colorless TMB to blue oxTMB through the •OH and O2 •- generated by the above Fenton-like reaction and strong peroxidase-like catalytic activities. ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic analysis of the Janus micro-composite
ce:section.ce:para:
The enzymatic reaction kinetic analysis of the Janus micro-composite for H2O2 and TMB was also evaluated under the optimum condition. The Michaelis constant (
k m

value, lower than HRP (0.43 mM and 3.7 mM), which also could confirm that our Janus micro-composite had a higher affinity for TMB and H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Janus micro-composite-assisted colorimetric determination of catechol
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the high peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the Janus micro-composite, a facile colorimetric detection system of catechol was established. As shown in Fig. 5 a, when the concentration of catechol increased, the color of the colorimetric system changed. When the concentration of catechol reached 100 μM, the blue solution changed to a colorless solution, demonstrating that oxTMB was reduced to TMB. Meanwhile, the UV absorbance values of the system at 652 nm were measured. As descripted in Fig. 5b, with the increase of catechol concentration, the UV absorption value at 652 nm continuously decreased to close to zero. The result was consisted with Fig. 5a. When the UV absorption value at 652 nm continuously dropped, the concentration of catechol reached 100 μM and was stable. Therefore, 100 μM was selected as the optimal colorimetric detection concentration of catechol. The concentration of catechol was positively correlated with absorbance at 652 nm. As shown in Fig. 5b, the linear equation was
Y = − 0.00604 X + 0.60327
(R2 = 0.997). The detection limit of catechol detected by Janus micro-composite was calculated by the formula (12):
(12)
LOD
= 3 ×
S D
k
Where, SD was the standard deviation of multiple tests in blank control group, k was the slope of calibration curve. The Janus micro-composite-assisted catechol detection limit (LOD) was obtained at 0.49 μM (S/N = 3) by Equation (12). Compared with those of the reported catechol detectors, the designed Janus micro-composite-assisted detection system could exhibit a lower detection limit, as shown in Table S2. This phenomenon might be attributed to the high sensitivity and selectivity of Janus micro-composite and its high peroxidase-like catalytic activity. At the same time, the autonomous driving behavior of the Janus micro-composite also could increase its mixing with pollutants, thus increasing the contact area and improving the detection efficiency. Based on the above results, such Janus micro-composite could be utilized as an efficient tool to detect organic pollutants catechol, providing a more convenient way for environmental remediation.

To further explore the high sensitivity and selectivity of the Janus micro-composite to catechol, the interference test was also evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5c and d, some phenols (2,4-DCP, phenol, resorcinol, catechol) and metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cl-, CO3 2−) were selected as possible interference substances. When these possible interfering substances were introduced into the colorimetric system, the color of blue oxTMB could hardly change. However, when catechol was added to the colorimetric system, the blue oxTMB instantly could became colorless. Simultaneously, the UV absorption curves of these colorimetric systems at 652 nm were also measured. Only when catechol was introduced into the system, the UV absorption value was almost zero. It was consistent with the above Fig. 5c, confirming the high selectivity and sensitivity of Janus micro-composite to catechol.

In addition, the detection mechanism of Janus micro-composite for catechol had also been further investigated. As descripted in Fig. 5e, when Janus micro-composite with peroxidase-like activity and Fenton-like reaction was introduced into TMB, on the one hand, H2O2 was efficiently decomposed into •OH under the acid condition. On the other hand, H2O2 was decomposed into O2 and H2O molecules. The electrons obtained by O2 molecules were further converted into unstable O2 •-. The formation of •OH and O2 •- could promote colorless TMB turn into blue oxTMB. When catechol was introduced into the color rendering system, oxTMB was transformed into TMB by the reducibility of the phenol hydroxyl group. The color of oxTMB changed to the colorless TMB. This phenomenon was essentially the process of giving or absorbing electrons. In addition, oxygen molecules could be used as a power source to drive the rapid rotation of Janus micro-composite, which could enhance the collision with oxTMB molecules, prompting the Janus micro-composite to attack catechol, and could also make the blue solution colorless, improving the sensitivity of colorimetric detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of catechol assisted by Janus micro-composite
ce:section.ce:para:
With the help of the high peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the Janus micro-composite, it could promote the efficient production of strong oxidizing hydroxyl radicals from hydrogen peroxide, and achieve the efficient attack of the Janus micro-composite on catechol, thus improving its high sensitivity detection. It inspired us to efficiently remove and degrade catechol in water with the help of the designed Janus micro-composite. The catalytic activity of peroxidase-like based on Janus micro-composite was related to pH in the Janus micro-composite. Therefore, the effects of pH in the Janus micro-composite on catechol degradation have also been investigated. As descripted in Fig. 6 a, when pH was 6.0 under weak acid condition, Janus micro-composite-assisted maximum catechol degradation rate reached 95.8%. However, the degradation rate of catechol reached the lowest 70.6% at a neutral pH of 7.0. It might be attributed to the release of a large amount of hydrogen ions under acidic conditions, which could promote the dissociation of catechol, form more free radicals and accelerate the degradation of catechol. Meanwhile, under acidic conditions, the Janus micro-composite had high peroxidase-like catalytic activity, and it could also improve the removal efficiency of catechol with the assistance of the Janus micro-composite. Moreover, the reduction of degradation efficiency was also due to the complex inhibition between excessive positive ions and anions in the degradation system and •OH. H2O2 could obtain a proton to gain H3O2 +, inhibit the formation of •OH, and reduce the removal efficiency. The specific process was shown in the reaction formula (13-14):
(13)
• OH +
H +
+
e −
→
H 2
O
(14)
H 2
O 2
+
H +
→
H 3
O 2
+

At the same time, the effects of stirring in the Janus micro-composite on catechol degradation were also investigated. As shown in Fig. 6b, the removal rate of catechol by the lac-MnO2@C@CuS Janus micro-composite could reach up 96.6% at 180 min without stirring, while the removal rate of with stirring in Janus micro-composite was not more than 70%, indicating that stirring affected the contact between the lac-MnO2@C@CuS Janus micro-composite and catechol and led to the decrease of the degradation efficiency (Xing et al., 2023). However, the removal rates of phenol, resorcinol and 2, 4-DCP were only 10.2%, 15.5% and 20.8% at 180 min, respectively (Fig. S4). In addition, with the extension of reaction time, the H2O2 content was continuously reduced using the H2O2 kit. Meanwhile, the velocity of Janus micro-composite was also decreased continuously (Fig. S5). Therefore, when the reaction had been carried out for 90 min, additional H2O2 was added to the degradation system to ensure the normal autonomous driving speed and the stable nanozyme-like catalytic activity of the Janus micro-composite.

The mechanism of catechol degradation by laccase immobilized Janus micro-composite was further analyzed. As shown in Fig. 6c, laccase was a kind of polyphenol oxidase containing copper. The copper ion in its structure could oxidize catechol. Laccase could catalyze the substitution of methoxy in the ortho-site of phenol hydroxyl group, which could then be added intramolecular into furan derivatives, and then degraded into small molecules by a series of free laccase. The large specific surface area and porous structure of CuS could improve the diffusion and mass transfer efficiency of small molecules, reduce the mass transfer resistance, and increase the contact probability of catechol substrate and the internal laccase macromolecule immobilized by the Janus micro-composite, which could better remove catechol in water. Moreover, CuS and C dots all could exhibit the high catalytic peroxidase activity, catalyzing the decomposition of H2O2 into •OH. On the one hand, MnO2 NPs (Mn4+/Mn2+) could decompose H2O2 into •OH by Fenton-like reaction. On the other hand, it could catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into O2, which could obtain electrons converted into O2 •-. Therefore, •OH and O2 •- obtained by multipath transformation could further attack catechol molecules to produce 1, 2-benzoquinone, which could be further oxidized to produce fatty acids and lipids through the benzene ring cleavage mechanism. Ester intermediates and fatty acids were the first to break chains under the attack of water molecules and active species such as •OH and O2 •-. As the hydrolysis process continued, the small molecular weight impurities could continue to degrade to form water-soluble degradation intermediates. These intermediates were eventually metabolized and mineralized by microorganisms to form carbon dioxide (CO2) and H2O molecules (Liu et al., 2022; J.C. Zhang et al., 2022; Tang et al., 2023; Jiang et al., 2023). To further confirm the generation of CO2 molecules, the total organic carbon content (TOC) was also analyzed (Fig. S6). The TOC removal rate reached 88.5% after 60 min. The complete degradation reaction of catechol could be exhibited by the following Equation 15 and 16:
(15)
• OH + HQ →
CO 2
+
H 2
O
(16)

• −
+ HQ →
CO 2

H 2
O

Compared with the existing industrial organic pollutant water treatment methods, the Janus micro-composite also has certain significance in the treatment of catechol in industrial wastewater. The introduction of Janus micro-composites into industrial wastewater can efficiently catalyze reactive oxygen species in wastewater to achieve autonomous movement. Simultaneous, it can promote the rapid mixing collision between Janus micro-composites and catechol, increase the contact area and opportunity, so as to obtain higher catalytic action time and improve removal efficiency. In addition, catechol is eventually degraded into non-toxic CO2, which is conducive to the self-repair of the environment and the recycling and discharge of industrial water. This could also provide new insights into practical remediation of industrial wastewater pollution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of laccase immobilized Janus micro-composite
ce:section.ce:para: The stability and reusable value of Janus micro-composite are critical factors for catechol degradation. Because the Janus micro-composite had strong peroxidase-like activity, the stability of Janus micro-composite was evaluated by colorimetric method with TMB as substrate. It could be observed that the Janus micro-composite still could keep high catalytic activity after four cycles (Fig. 7 a). Meanwhile, the colour of the four colorimetric systems still maintained unchanged. The absorbance value of the colorimetric system at 652 nm barely changed after 4 cycles and the degradation efficiency was above 90%, indicating that the peroxidase-like activity of the Janus micro-composite did not decrease and the micro-composite had good catalytic stability (Fig. 7b). Furthermore, the degradation efficiency could still reach more than 70% after 20 cycles (Fig. S7), indicating that the Janus micro-composite has the potential for sustainable industrial applications due to their efficient removal and stability. It was assumed that because the metal ions on the surface of the Janus micro-composite will partially overflow, resulting in the degradation of the catalyst performance. In addition, some MnO2 and laccase would inevitably be lost in the process of catalyst washing and re-collection after each cycle test. The experimental results showed that the Janus micro-composite had good stability in aqueous solution, and could show an excellent reusability in four reuse experiments, indicating that the prepared Janus micro-composite had certain practical utilization value and practical significance for the detection and degradation of catechol. To further explore the morphology and crystallinity changes of the Janus micro-composite after degradation, XRD patterns before and after degradation were further analyzed in Fig. 7c. The used Janus micro-composite maintained the characteristic peaks of MnO2 (PDF#12–0141) and CuS (PDF#06–0464) contained in the original micro-composite, which could prove that the designed micro-composite had an excellent chemical stability. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 7d, the SEM image showed that the recovered Janus micro-composite still could maintain the original morphology after four cycles and preserve the spherical particle structure of the nano-flower, confirming the high stability and recyclability of the Janus micro-composite.

In summary, the laccase immobilized Janus micro-composite is constructed via the hydrothermal method and photo-reduction method. As a new type of intelligent platform, the Janus micro-composite can simultaneously provide practical significance for the detection and degradation of catechol. The asymmetrical distribution of MnO2 NPs on the semi-surface of the Janus micro-composite is realized by a simple photo-assisted reduction half-coating method, and the Janus micro-composite can achieve autonomous propulsion at a speed of 178.83 ± 2.07 μm/s. The Janus micro-composite exhibits inherent peroxidase-like enzyme that catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to produce •OH, and can dynamically detect catechol with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.49 μM and a linear range of 0–100 μM, showing excellent selectivity and sensitivity. With the help of H2O2, the degradation rate of catechol reached more than 90% in 180 min. The Janus micro-composite is absorbed on the surface of H2O2 and can generate a large number of •OH, and then the substance TMB is oxidized to oxTMB. In addition, the introduction of catechol can promote the process of the reaction and the generation of •OH, thus realizing the detection of catechol. A large number of active species are generated during the detection and degradation of micro-actuators, which synergistically act on pollutants and thus degrade organic pollutant molecules quickly and efficiently. Although the assembly of materials is currently complex, the sensitive colorimetric detection and excellent degradation efficiency of catechol via this nanoplatform provides a new perspective for environmental remediation in real life and industry.
Shuxing Zhou: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Yin Wang: Data curation, Formal analysis. Wenyu Cao: Formal analysis.
